Preface

This study guide was written by Harper L., with consultation from Desmond L. Writing that is italicized is me breaking the flow of the guide to provide commentary or additional information that might be outside of the scope of the terms I am defining. This study guide was created with the purpose of helping prepare for the 2025 version of the Pearson/Certiport IT Specialist Networking Exam, (OD-101) but I believe this knowledge will be useful to have for many other exams like Network+ or CCST or whatever else. I am one person, and I am going off the objectives, not the test itself. This study guide is not comprehensive: there are terms that may appear on the test that are not listed within this guide and there may be terms that are in the guide that are not on the test.

Usage Guide

I recommend that you do not read this whole document in one sitting. It took me a couple of days to write this, and it will probably take longer to read. I suggest that a reader have this guide handy when using other study material, such as the GMetrix practice tests, and when something comes up that you do not know, find it in the guide. I also recommend that you use this guide in tandem with GMetrix’s study guide feature, so that you can see what objectives you need improvement on, and you can study up on them here. This way you won’t read the whole thing for no reason, there should be stuff you already know. Please do not use this guide as a crutch. Try to complete the GMetrix practices in testing mode without the guide and only refer to it when using the training mode or when reviewing missed questions. There is not much available to study for these tests as they are relatively new, but YouTube is your friend for millions of videos explaining things I failed to: Check out Professor Messer and PowerCert Animated Videos, and the website geeksforgeeks.org is very informative. You could even use… AI…to create practice questions for yourself, since you wouldn’t be using it for cheating or plagiarizing.

Extra Notices

I unfortunately do not have the original sources I used for gathering more information nor for the pictures, but I definitely used geeksforgeeks.org as well as Certiport for the objectives list. View the objectives at:

certiport.filecamp.com/s/i/ITS_Networking

Good luck!

1. Networking Fundamentals

    1. 1.1Define Network Concepts 

Internet – The largest collection of interconnected networks and datacenters available for public use.

Intranet – An organization based private network that is held within organization-managed devices and networks. An example of this would be the a public school district domain where students log into managed computers with their student ID and domain name.

Extranet – A middle ground between an intranet and a public network. An extranet only allows communication between specifically allowed organizations such as business partners or for collaborations between school systems.

Client-Server – A networking model where client devices (Like computers and mobile devices) request files or resources from a server (More powerful computer designed for a specific task, like file hosting or email services)

Peer-To-Peer – A networking model where each device is considered equal and can both send files to other devices as well as ask for files from other devices.

 

Transmission Types

Unicast – A type of network transmission where a node only sends a file or piece of information to one recipient.

Multicast – A type of network transmission where a node sends information to multiple destination nodes.

Broadcast – A type of network transmission where a node sends information to all devices on the network segment.

Anycast – A network transmission that involves both routing and addressing its systems. Multiple servers will share an IP address, and a router will get information from the closest server to the client from that one shared IP address.

Internet of Things (IoT) – The Internet of Things is a term that refers to usually non-network-capable devices (think things in your home like fridges, televisions, HVAC systems, CCTV) that are connected to the Internet and are considered “smart devices.” This is mostly for automation of tasks like an IoT thermometer being able to sense changes in weather and adjust accordingly, or your security cameras can be controlled by an app on your phone. Remember, each IoT device must have an IP address in your home network to be considered as such.

    1. 1.2Define cloud and virtualization concepts 

Hypervisor – A type of software that allows multiple “Virtual Machines” to run on one set of hardware. There are two types: A type I hypervisor, also known as a bare metal hypervisor, runs directly on physical hardware and has access to hardware directly. A Type II hypervisor, known as a hosted hypervisor, is a program run within an installed OS that allows for virtualization (Examples are Oracle VirtualBox or VMware running within an actual Windows 11 operating system.)

Virtual Machine – A device that is emulated through the use of software or through Type I hypervisors. I pretty much defined both terms under hypervisor.

Virtual Switch – A virtual switch or VSwitch, is a software-based version of a layer 2 switch, which is set up so that your virtual machines can communicate with each other as if they were physically connected.

    1. 1.3Describe remote access methods 

Virtual Private Network (VPN) – A technology where an encrypted private tunnel is created over the Internet between two bodies, most commonly between a user’s device and a network at a school or workplace. There are two common types based on the bodies that are being connected.

Host-To-Site – A VPN where a device like a single computer or phone is connected to a faraway network like if an employee is working from home.

Site-To-Site – A VPN where an entire network is connected to another over the Internet. An example would be two businesses partnering up would use a VPN to connect their two resources, similar in use to an Extranet.

Remote Desktop – A technology that uses TCP/UDP port 3389 to connect one user’s desktop to another through a program and the Internet. This is commonly used by tech support workers to help solve issues without verbally guiding them over the phone. Be careful to only connect and allow connections you trust.

  1. 2.Network Infrastructures 

    1. 2.1Define the characteristics of local area networks (LANs) 

Local Area Network (LAN) – A network that is typically physically connected through cabling, usually at the scope of a home, office, or campus.

Inside (Trusted Zone) – A security zone that encompasses your LAN or is set up at the Intranet level. The sites you visit within this are fully trusted.

Outside (Untrusted Zone) – A security zone that refers to websites on the public Internet, usually these are not trusted by default and must be added to the trusted zone through whitelisting.

DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) – A security zone that is a buffer between trusted and untrusted zones. It is often for public facing services like web and email servers that will need to be accessed via the Internet.

Management Zone – An optional security zone that is used to isolate network management systems like software firewalls. It is a secure place to manage network infrastructure.

Virtual LAN (VLAN) – A virtual LAN is a software-based LAN configured on a router, which allows devices that aren’t physically connected to communicate through Internet Protocol.

Wireless LAN (WLAN) – A LAN that is connected wirelessly. This is why I mention that LANs are most commonly physically connected but they technically don’t have to be, you can imagine your home is probably not connected with wiring, it might be interconnected privately through a router or access point which allows LAN functionality without physical connections.

    1. 2.2Define the characteristics of wide area networks (WANs) 

Wide Area Network (WAN) – A network that usually expands across multiple buildings or sites, think of a WAN as a business having a WAN that connects their branch office in Japan with the offices in the United States and Canada.

Dial Up – A very old technology (1989) that uses a 56-kilobit modem that has to call into a telephone network, using audio signals and sounds as data that is interpreted by a modem and router which connects you to the Internet. Max throughput of 56 Kbps.

Integrated Services Digital Network (ISDN) – A technology that is one year older than Dial Up (1988) and yet still manages to be faster and more reliable. It uses two 64 Kbps channels to achieve a max data rate of 128 Kbps. Both Dial Up and ISDN are being phased out (as if they haven’t already been phased out) and are replaced by DSL.

Digital Subscriber Line (DSL) – A WAN technology where existing phone lines are connected from an Internet Service Provider to a DSL modem. While phone lines are existing older technology used for land line telephones, now they are purposed to provide Internet access and are still a widely used way for people to use the Internet. DSL reaches max speeds up to 100 Mbps

Cable Internet and Cable Modem – A cable modem is a modem that uses a coaxial cable (the same cable used for cable television) to connect from the cable provider to the devices in a home network. Cable Internet is typically included in a package from a cable provider like Cox. Cable Internet can reach speeds up to 1 Gbps, but keep in mind that your bandwidth is shared across others in your neighborhood so speeds may vary.

Satellite – Satellite Internet requires a physical satellite dish to be installed to your home. Satellite Internet is not used much in urban or city areas, it is mostly used in remote areas with little existing infrastructure. Satellite Internet is susceptible to latency and delays, and weather conditions can affect the functionality of your service.

Cellular – Your device will connect to a cellular tower based on your geographic location; you must have a cell phone plan most likely to use this type of connection.

    1. 2.3Identify wireless networking methods and characteristics 

802.11 – IEEE’s standard for wireless networking.

Name

Adoption Date

Frequency Bands Supported

Maximum Throughput Rate

802.11b

1999

2.4 GHz

11 Mbps

802.11a

1999

5 GHz

54 Mbps

802.11g

2003

2.4 GHz

54 Mbps

802.11n (Wi-Fi 4)

2009

2.4, 5 GHz

600 Mbps

802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5)

2013

5 GHz

6.93 Gbps

802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6)

2021

2.4, 5, 6 GHz

9.6 Gbps

802.11be (Wi-Fi 7)

2024

2.4, 5, 6 GHz

46 Gbps

 

Bluetooth – A technology for devices connecting with each other using radio frequency. Similar to NFC and RFID. Also known as a Personal Area Network (PAN) and defined by the IEEE 802.15 standard.

Network Security Types – There are different types of network security for wireless networks, the most secure and current one is WPA3, here is a list of the types and their characteristics:

Name

 

Adoption Date

Security Level

Description and Characteristics

WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy)

1997

Very Low

WEP used a static key that was shared across all devices connected to the network to encrypt and decrypt data. It also used this key for authentication. Obviously, it is very insecure and exploitable now simply because it is old and deprecated.

WPA (Wi-Fi Protected Access)

2003

Low

WPA used Temporal Key Integrity Protocol (TKIP) which simply was an improvement over WEP’s weaker encryption methods. The gist of TKIP is that it encrypted each packet with a unique key rather than using one key for nearly everything.

WPA2

2004

Moderate

WPA2 is a big improvement to WPA1. It uses the Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) encryption algorithm instead of TKIP. In short, AES is still considered very secure, and WPA2 is still widely used today.

WPA3

2018

High

WPA3 is newer and more secure, but it is not widely used due to compatibility reasons. WPA3 works on Wi-Fi 6 and above.

 

802.1x – This is an IEEE standard created for authentication of devices connecting to a network. It requires a device to be authenticated when being plugged into a LAN or WLAN before being able to access the protected part of the network. Basically, when a device plugs into a switch or accesses a wireless network, a request is sent through an authenticator device (which is a switch or access point that connects between the device and the “RADIUS Server”) to a server that receives and responds to the request. Typically, a user needs to provide some sort of authentication credentials, such as a username and password or a digital certificate, and that gets passed to the authentication server which gives you access to the network.

Point-to-Point (P2P) – A direct connection between two devices.

Ad-Hoc – “When necessary or needed” A wireless connection model that is decentralized, meaning it has no specific central forwarding device or central control device, so no access points or routers, devices connect to each other.

Infrastructure (Regarding wireless connection models) – A centralized device like a router, or access point is in the middle of all devices and all devices connect to that. This could also refer to when devices connect to cell towers when using cellular.

Wireless Bridging – Connecting two devices as a P2P using a dedicated wireless bridge device or a router that is configured to “bridge mode.” Wireless bridging can be used to connect multiple 802.11 standard networks together.

Wireless Interference – When a wireless network experiences decreased strength, speed, connectivity, because of outside factors like Bluetooth devices causing problems, overlapping radio waves, microwave devices. It is the wireless equivalent to EMI for a wired network.

Before this next section I would like to attach a picture of the hierarchy of Area Networks, in order from smallest to largest in size and scope.

Important ones to know: PAN (Personal Area Network, Bluetooth), LAN (Local Area Network, Ethernet Switches), CAN (Campus Area Network, Colleges) MAN (Metropolitan Area Network, Cities), WAN (Large geographical locations varying in size, Internet is an example)

    1. 2.4Compare and contrast network topologies and access methods 

This section covers network topologies, both physical and logical. I will go over the difference between physical and logical, then define each topology in the order I see fit which will be a mixing and matching of how current they are and how effective they are.

 

The difference between a physical and logical topology is that a physical is the physical connections

and layout of the network nodes, but a logical topology is how devices communicate and how data

flows within the network: A network can have a physical topology that is different from its logical topology, not to mention VLANs and similar configurations.

 

Bus Topology – One of the more outdated topologies, all devices are connected (typically in a straight line) to a shared cable. This means if one device has a network problem, or one part of this bus cabling system goes out, the entire network shuts down. It’s like Christmas lights. Usually, this topology involves using BNC connectors, which are connectors that you see on coaxial cables (Cable TV connectors) and the main cabling line must start and end with “terminators” to prevent continuous reflection of signals and interference.

Ring Topology – Similar to a bus topology but the devices are connected in a circle. Each computer is connected to two other systems at least.

Star Topology – An improved LAN topology where each device in the network is connected to a “central forwarding device” which could be a network hub, switch, or router, and sometimes multiple of these are combined depending on the desired network functionality. The great thing about star topologies is that if one cable goes down, it doesn’t take down the entire network, it only affects the function of two interfaces. Star topologies are also easy to configure and set up. The flaw with this type of network is that if the central forwarding device runs into a problem or goes down, the entire network connected to that device stops working. The larger version of this topology that is used for WANs is called “hub and spoke.”

Hub and Spoke – This topology is similar to star, but is larger in scope and is higher level, usually regarding WANs instead of LANs. All the networks in each office are connected to the data center.

Mesh Topology – This topology is the most difficult and expensive to set up. Each device is connected to every other device on the network. In execution, this means that every device has a direct path to any other device on the network. It is known as “self-healing” because a bad cable doesn’t mean much: devices can use other devices to create an alternate route to their destination. It is also considered the most reliable, because it takes a lot to bring a mesh network down. The Internet is supposedly a combination of mesh and hub and spoke topologies. Since full mesh is so expensive to set up, most businesses or buildings have partial mesh, where the most important devices are set up to use full mesh while less important devices use less connections.

Before I continue to the next section, I want to go over T1, E1, T3 and E3.

T1 – An American dedicated WAN connection technology that uses wiring to connect your building to an ISP. A t1 connection uses 24 64 Kbps channels for a practical data rate of 1.536 Mbps, though it is advertised as 1.544, but 8 kilobits are used for framing and synchronization.

E1 – A European version of T1. It uses 32 64 Kbps channels to reach a throughput rate of 2.048 Mbps

T3 – A combination of 28 T1 lines together for high-speed internet access up to 45 Mbps. Also known as DS3.

E3 – A combination of 16 E1 lines together for a total throughput rate of 34.368 Mbps.

 

These are known as leased lines because they are specifically private and paid for between an organization and the carrier.

 

  1. 3.Network Hardware 

    1. 3.1Describe characteristics of switches 

Hub – An older network device that takes data and forwards it to every device connected to its ports, this takes up bandwidth and is slower and less efficient. It also sends data to every device regardless of whether they need it or not.

Switch – A network device that uses a table of MAC addresses to determine where to forward data. It is similar to a hub, but it is able to use MAC addresses to only forward data to the desired destination. If it does not know where to forward data it will act like a hub and send the data through all ports, but it most likely will not do that very much.

Switch Ports – There are three types of ports on a network switch. An access port connects a switch to an end device like a computer or a printer. A voice access port connects the switch to a VoIP phone device such as the classic Cisco IP Phone devices. A trunk port connects the switch to another forwarding device like another switch or a router.

Managed vs Unmanaged Switches – Unmanaged Switches are not managed by network administrators and have little proprietary rules and protections. Managed switches are able to be configured by network administrators from configuration consoles or from an IT management application. Rules can be added to switches (802.1x) to prevent people from just plugging into a switch and having full access to the LAN.

Layer 2 vs Layer 3 Switches – Layers 2 and 3 refer to the Data Link and Network layers of the OSI model. (Please look that up if you do not know what it is, you will use it throughout your career.) Layer 2 Switches are more traditional switches, using MAC addresses to determine where to send ethernet frames of data. Layer 3 switches use IP addresses to determine destinations and can route between different LANs and VLANs, it operates at layer 3 as the name implies. Layer 2 switches use a “MAC table” and layer 3 switches use a “Routing table.”

Duplex – This refers to the ability of a switch or cable to send data both ways, or at the same time, etc. Simplex means data can only travel one way and one at a time. Half-Duplex means data can travel from either end of the cable, but only one at a time. Full Duplex means data can travel from both sides at the same time.

Switch Forwarding Methods – There are 3 switch forwarding methods that need to be known for the test. Store-and-forward means a switch has to receive an ethernet frame in its entirety and must complete error checking on it before sending it to the destination, this means this method has the best reliability that valid data is being sent. Cut-Through (Also known as fast-forward) means that the switch only needs to receive the destination MAC address (first 6 bytes) before it can make a forwarding decision, which means it is faster than store-and-forward, but there can be unreliability. Fragment-free is a middle ground (though it is technically another version of cut-through) where a switch needs to examine the first 64 bytes of a frame. 64 bytes is the minimum size for an ethernet frame which is why this method reads up to 64 before making a forwarding decision.

Collision Domain – This means a network segment where data frames or packets can potentially collide when two transmissions are made simultaneously.

CSMA/CD – Carrier Sense Multiple Access Collision Detection is a method to manage data packet or ethernet frame collisions, where it waits for the transmission channel to be idle before attempting to transmit. If a collision happens, both devices wait a random amount of time before trying again.

CSMA/CA – Carrier Sense Multiple Access Collision Avoidance is an improved collision prevention method where it listens to the channel, and if it is busy, it waits before trying to send. This avoids collisions more than CD because devices using CSMA/CD will transmit immediately upon hearing that the channel is idle, while CSMA/CA will wait if the channel is busy which usually means transmissions upon the channel becoming idle again will not be happening at the same exact time.

Broadcast Domain – A network segment where all devices can receive broadcast packets from a device. Think about collision domains as a LAN using a hub, where every device in a collision domain must pay attention to the data received regardless of if they needed it or not. Broadcast domains are collision domains that only pay attention to data received from a broadcast.

Spanning Tree Protocol – This is a protocol for switches that prevents looping and “broadcast storms” when there are multiple paths exist between devices. Since there is no “Time to Live” for ethernet frames, they can be sent out infinitely between switches that are connected together. Spanning Tree Protocol assigns different roles to ports so that one port is “disabled” and not used for forwarding, and one port may be used for dedicated forwarding between one switch to another.

    1. 3.2 

    1. 3.2Describe Characteristics of routers 

Network Bottleneck – A point in a network where data flow is limited and slowed due to limited resources, lowered capacity, or lesser technology.

Directly Connected Routes – These are routes where the routers are directly connected and active. These have an administrative distance of 0.

Static Routing - A configuration of routers where the routes are explicitly defined and preconfigured by administrators with fixed values. This can allow administrators to create their own forwarding routes which will be followed by the devices.

Dynamic Routing – Uses protocols and real time data and states of devices to determine a proper route for data to flow.

Distance Vector vs Link-State – Distance Vector routing protocols send information to their directly connected neighbors, and the routes are determined by distance. Link-State routing protocols make sure the entire network has the same view of the logical network topology.

Routing Information Protocol (RIP) – A distance vector routing protocol that uses “hops” to determine the best path between the source network and the destination network.  The route cannot exceed 15 hops, basically meaning that the number of routers between the source network router and its destination is 15.

Interior Gateway Protocol (IGP) – A type of routing protocol used for exchanging routing information tables between networks within a single autonomous system.

Exterior Gateway Protocol (EGP) – A type of routing protocol used for exchanging routing information tables between networks between multiple autonomous systems.

Routing Table – A database used by routers to determine the best path for forwarding packets.

Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) – A link-state routing protocol that uses an algorithm to determine the best shortest routing path.

Port Forwarding – A protocol that allows outside requests from the Internet to be received by a router using its IP address and an external port number that gets read by the router and sent to a corresponding IP private address. It is configured on the router specifically. For example, a router may see a request with the port number 192.168.1.1:3389, and then it will send the request to the internal IP address of 192.168.1.2 through port forwarding. For more information watch the PowerCert Animated Videos video about Port Forwarding.

Quality of Service (QoS) – A technology that allows prioritization of certain network traffic over others, ensuring reliability and a better connection. This is usually configured on network devices like routers. QoS can allocate bandwidth to certain applications or traffic through marking data packets with a priority level, and they use queues to manage the flow of traffic. For example, you can use QoS to prioritize your video conference call over something like your other open applications and email systems.

Network Segmentation – Separating networks into smaller segments. This is done with routers by configuring VLANs, or it can be implemented with firewalls. It is used to increase security and reduce the risk and consequences of a network breach.

Convergence – A state where routers share the same updated information about the current logical topology. Each router in a network segment has the same idea regarding the current layout of the network.

    1. 3.3Describe characteristics of physical media 

Twisted Pair Copper Wiring – Uses a pair of two copper wires twisted to avoid interference. This is probably the most common wiring that is used in computers, it is used with Ethernet cables and is uses an RJ-45 connector. RJ-45 is known as 8p8c for its 8 pins and 8 wires, as opposed to RJ-11 which is for phone line connections, known as 6p2c.

Coaxial Cable – A type of cable that is used for cable television, which also means it is used for cable internet. They are typically used for connecting a cable television box to a television, but in the context of networking they are used to connect a cable modem to a wall plate that is installed by a cable company such as Cox. The most common connectors for coaxial are known as BNC (Bayonet Neill-Concelman) and F-Type, which is more popular for internet connections. Coaxial is not seen much now compared to copper and fiber, but it is still good to be able to spot it.

Fiber Optic Cable – This wiring uses pulsing light signals instead of electrical signals, therefore it is basically immune to electromagnetic interference, also known as EMI. It is also considered more secure over copper wiring in terms of the way data is transmitted. There are many fiber optic connectors, the most popular are in the picture below. Fiber is more expensive than coaxial or copper, so you most likely will see it being used in large business buildings and facilities instead of a home network. Single Mode fiber uses one core and is for long distances, it is more expensive and less flexible. Multi-Mode Fiber uses multiple cores and is shorter distance, more flexible, and costs less.

Types of Shielding for Twisted Pair Copper Wire – There are combinations of the following types that you can learn more about through CompTIA learn Network+, and Professor Messer probably has a video about it. Here are the most basic types of shielding with no combinations.

Unshielded

Known as UTP, there is no shielding against EMI or crosstalk and the only protection comes from the fact the wires are twisted together, it provides the least protection against EMI and crosstalk.

Foiled

Known as FTP. It has a foil layer under the insulation. It provides more protection against EMI and crosstalk than UTP.

Fully Shielded

Known as STP. It has both a foil covering under the insulation and shielding over each wiring pair. It provides even more protection against interference and crosstalk than FTP and UTP.

 

Categories of copper wiring – This test only requires that you know the Cat Cable Standards from Cat5 up to Cat7. I am omitting some specifications, like frequency and the type of shielding supported. 1000BASE-T is known as Gigabit Ethernet, and the G in 10G stands for gigabit. GG-45 and TERA are two connectors that are used for Cat7, which to me is strange because Cat 8 (Class I) uses RJ-45 which I believe to be much easier and probably cheaper to buy.

Category

Ethernet Standard

Max Distance

Connector

Cat 5

100BASE-T

100 Meters

RJ-45

Cat 5e

1000BASE-T

100 Meters

RJ-45

Cat 6

1000BASE-T

or 10GBASE-T

100 Meters

55 Meters

RJ-45

Cat 6a

10GBASE-T

100 Meters

RJ-45

Cat 7

10GBASE-T

100 Meters

GG-45/TERA

Configuration of copper wiring (crossover vs straight-through) – This refers to the way the wires on copper wiring are laid out, either crossover (where the wires are crossed over each other) or straight-through, where the wires are straight. It is easier explained with the following photos. Crossover is used for connecting the same devices together, such as connecting two switches together or two computers together, or even the same types of devices (switches are connected to hubs through crossover). Straight-through is for connecting two different devices together, like a switch to a computer. There is another configuration known as rollover, which is for plugging into an administration port on a managed switch.

Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) – EMI is where electromagnetic waves from power cables, fluorescent lights, motors, fans, or even radio transmitters interfere with the signal travelling along copper wiring. This can cause signal loss, slower throughput, and can affect the integrity of data. This is why it is important to make sure cabling is not installed close to these sources of EMI.

Crosstalk – When one wire in a cable causes interference with another, which causes slower signal speed or intermittent loss of connection. There are many types of crosstalk which you can find out about online.

  1. 4.Protocols and services 

4.1 Describe the Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) Model

Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) Model – The OSI Model is an integral part of discussing the way data is processed and transmitted by computers, and it is used by many people in the IT field. It is a 7-layer non-concrete framework for how networks function. It goes from physical wiring and 1s and 0s all the way to what the user sees from their applications like a web browser or email client.

 

Layer

Function

  1. 1.Physical 

 

“Cables and bits”

The physical layer is all about the physical connections and computer hardware. This layer deals with raw bits of 1s and 0s being transferred over wires. Physical media would be the type of cables being used, connectors, analog or digital signals that represent bits. Parts of Ethernet reside in this layer, but it is mainly operational on layer 2.

  1. 2.Data Link 

 

“MAC addresses and Ethernet frames”

The data link layer is split into two sublayers: The Media Access Control (MAC) sublayer, and the Logical Link Control (LLC) sublayer. The MAC sublayer provides interfacing between two devices on a LAN using MAC addresses. (a unique address permanently assigned to a network interface card by the manufacturer that is a grouping of 12 hexadecimal digits like 84-A9-3E-75-A0-12) The MAC sublayer is also responsible for encapsulating the raw bits from the physical layer into ethernet “frames” which are processed into packets at layer 3. The LLC sublayer handles the logic of the frames, running error checking and managing the flow of frames making sure the frames are valid before they are made into packets. The LLC sublayer interfaces with the network layer, and Ethernet primarily operates in layer 2.

  1. 3.Network 

 

“IP addresses and packets”

The network layer manages logical addressing using IP addresses. Routers operate at this layer and routing protocols like RIP update the routing tables here. Packets are created from frames and are segmented into smaller pieces at this layer when needed, then they are routed from source IP address to destination IP address.

  1. 4.Transport 

 

“Reliable/fast delivery of data”

The transport layer delivers packet segments to their destination. There are two main protocols for this process, TCP and UDP. Transmission Control Protocol is a connection-based protocol that checks segments for errors and sends them in order, while also verifying that the segments reached their recipient. In contrast, User Datagram Protocol is connectionless. There is no error checking, and no ordering of segments, nor is there any verification that the segments even reached their destination. In short, TCP is connection-oriented and reliable, while UDP is connectionless and prioritizes speed over accuracy. TCP is used for services like email, webpages, and file transfers, because accuracy really matters for that. UDP would be used for services where there can be flaws, such as video streaming, online gaming, or voice chat.

  1. 5.Session 

 

“Allowing applications to talk to each other”

The session layer establishes, manages, and terminates connections between applications to provide a way for them to talk to each other. Sessions are logical connections between communicating apps and are created and synchronized at this layer. For example, using a voice chat service like Discord or video chat application like Zoom, the session layer keeps you connected with the other participants in the call and will attempt to reconnect you to the session if there is a temporary outage. TCP is assisted by the session layer by creating a session between the two apps for reliable data delivery.

  1. 6.Presentation 

 

“Encoding and encryption”

The presentation layer handles encryption and encoding which enables the data to be represented in a human readable form. An example of encoding would be ASCII, which converts binary values into characters from a character set. (e.g. binary 1100001 is 97, which represents lowercase A.) A more widely used encoding standard is Unicode (UTF-8) which allows for representation of many more symbols, multiple languages, and emojis. Other files like audio and video are encoded using different file formats and processes. Secure Socket Layer (SSL) and Transport Security Layer (TLS) operate at this layer and provide the encryption and security when using applications.

  1. 7.Application 

The application layer is the layer that interacts with a human when using network services. An example of this would be a web browser providing the user with a web page using HTTPS or using an FTP client to perform file transferring. Email clients like Gmail or Outlook use simple mail transfer protocol (SMTP) to send emails to their recipients, who then can view the message from a server (IMAP) or have it downloaded to their computer. (POP3) Two extensively used protocols at the application layer are Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol, which automatically assigns IP addresses to network nodes (it utilizes layers 2 and 3, but it is considered its own application) and Domain Name System, which allows users to type in a domain name like google.com, then translates that domain name into a destination IP address for HTTPS.

For more information on the OSI model, visit osi-model.com

    1. 4.2Describe the Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) model 

Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) Model – Known as the TCP/IP model, it is a more modern version of the OSI model, which combines some of the layers together for simplicity reasons. It has 4 layers instead of 7.

Layer

Function

  1. 1.Network Access 

The network access layer is a combination of the Physical and Data Link Layer. This layer regards itself to the physical connections and cables and hardware, as well as bytes traveling across the wires. Additionally, layer 1 handles MAC addressing and error checking as well as encapsulation of bytes into an ethernet frame. Ethernet operates at this layer. Switches, Hubs, and NIC cards function here.

  1. 2.Internet 

The internet layer is the network layer. It handles IP addressing and routing protocols. Routers operate here.

  1. 3.Transport 

The transport layer is the same as in the OSI model. It handles data segments and sends them either in order with error checking, connected to the recipient through TCP or sends packets fast and connectionless with UDP.

  1. 4.Application 

The application layer of the TCP/IP model combines the session, presentation, and application layers. Therefore, this layer handles creating and managing sessions between applications, encoding and encryption to make data presentable and understandable, and it handles user interaction through many application protocols.

For more information on the TCP model, visit geeksforgeeks.org/tcp-ip-model or wikipedia.org/wiki/Internet_protocol_suite

Data being transmitted goes from the top layer to the bottom layer, and vice versa. Here is a picture of the protocol data units (PDUs) at each layer.

    1. 4.3Describe IPv4 concepts 

IP Address (IPv4) – A logical (non-permanent) address that is either automatically assigned to a device through DHCP or manually assigned by an administrator or technician (known as a static IP). It is a 32-bit binary sequence grouped into 4 “octets” represented in decimal form. An example of an IPv4 address would be (192.168.100.1)

Subnet Mask – A group of 4 octets that represent which portion of the IP address is the network portion and which is the host portion. A 255 means that the corresponding part of the IP address is dedicated to a network, while a 0 means it identifies hosts. An IP address of 192.168.100.1 with a subnet mask of 255.255.255.0 means that the first 3 octets are the network and the last octet is the host. Therefore, the network is 192.168.100.0 and the hosts are represented with the last number (usually 1-254, as 255 is reserved for broadcast address.) Subnetting is usually for segmenting networks to be more manageable, and it also increases security.

Classful IP addressing – An old method of network configuration, it uses fixed subnets based on IP address classes, which in turn is based on the leading octet of the IP address.

Class

Range

Default Subnet Mask

A

0.0.0.0 – 126.255.255.255

255.0.0.0

B

128.0.0.0 – 191.255.255.255

255.255.0.0

C

192.0.0.0 – 223.255.255.255

255.255.255.0

D (Reserved for Multicasting)

224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255

N/A

E (Experimental)

240.0.0.0 – 254.255.255.255

N/A

127 is not used because it is reserved for loopback addresses.

Loopback Address – Usually 127.0.0.1, it is the address that refers to the machine that is being used. You can communicate with yourself using this address. It is also referred to as the localhost and is mostly used for diagnostic testing.

 

Classless IP addressing – A newer method of network IP address configuration. Most subnetting is denoted with CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) where an IP address is given with a slash and a number to represent how many bits the network portion is. 192.168.100.1 /24 means that the Network portion is the first 24 bits (3 octets, meaning 255.255.255.0 subnet) and the remaining octet is for hosts.

Default Gateway – The default gateway is the default forwarding host, or the default point for an IP packet to be routed to if it no other route matches the destination IP address specified.

Socket Address – A socket address is an IP address combined with a port number, something like 192.168.1.8:443 where the IP address and port number are separated by a colon. It is used in port forwarding and allows specific communication between two applications.

Broadcast Address – The broadcast address is an address that will send data to all devices on the local network you are connected to. 255.255.255.255 is the local broadcast address which sends data to all hosts on the same broadcast domain. Typing the network ID followed by 255 (example 192.168.1.255 with subnet 255.255.255.0) is a directed broadcast which allows you to target a certain subnet. Only directed broadcasts can be routed to another network segment.

Private vs Public IP addresses – Private IP addresses are local to a LAN and cannot access the Internet. Public IP addresses are able to be used for the Internet. Private IP addresses can access the Internet through something called Network Address Translation (NAT) Here are common private IP ranges.

Class A Private IP range – 10.0.0.0 to 10.255.255.255

Class B Private IP range – 172.16.0.0 to 172.31.255.255

Class C Private IP range – 192.168.0.0 to 192.168.255.255

    1. 4.4Describe IPv6 concepts 

IPv6 Address – A 128-bit network address made out of hexadecimal digits and is separated by a colon instead of a period. IPv6 is used because we will eventually run out of IPv4 addresses due to the increasing creation of IoT devices and more people gaining access to the Internet and such.

Example address: 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334

Subnet Mask – A slash is used to indicate how many bits are used, similar to CIDR. /64 at the end of an IPv6 address means 64 bits out of the 128 are used for the network ID, and the rest are for the host ID.

Default Gateway – The default gateway is the same as in IPv4, except it is a Link-Local address. It is the default point to where packets can be routed. The typical default gateway would be fe80::1.

Socket Address – An IPv6 address with a port number attached at the end. Port numbers are the same with both versions of IP. Since IPv6 uses colons to separate the groups of bits, socket addresses have their IP portions enclosed in brackets, with the port number at the end. [2001:db8:0::1]:80

Truncation/Abbreviation – IPv6 addresses can be shortened because… they are incredibly long and difficult to read compared to IPv4. You can remove a long string of zeroes that will be shown as two colons side by side, you can only do this once. (::) You can also remove one group of zeroes that is in between two groups of bits so that only one zero in the group remains (2001:0000:db8a  becomes 2001:0:db8a) Leading zeroes that come before a number in a group can be removed also. Like 0003:0004 will become 3:4.

Tunneling Protocols – Protocols that allow IPv6 traffic to be transported over IPv4 networks. Examples are GRE, Teredo, ISATAP, 6in4, and 6to4.

Tunnel Brokers – A service that provides an endpoint for IPv6 to be able to be transmitted over an IPv4 network.

Dual IP stack – A network configuration method where devices are able to support both IPv4 and IPv6 at the same time.

Link-Local vs Global – Link-Local addresses are used for communication in a network segment and is not routable over the Internet. It is like a private IPv6 address. Global addresses can be routed over the Internet and are basically public IP addresses. The prefix is FE80::  with a subnet mask of /10

Multicast Groups – Groups where one multicast will be sent to each device using the IP address. Range is (FF00::/8)

Loopback – The loopback address is the address a computer uses to communicate with itself for diagnostic purposes. The IPv6 loopback address is ::1 which is in function the same as 127.0.0.1.

    1. 4.5Identify well-known ports 

These are the ports that are listed in the objectives, ones not listed may appear on the exam.

Protocol

Port Number

Description

HTTP

80

Hyper Text Transfer Protocol, the default protocol for returning webpages when using a web browser. It is considered insecure now.

HTTPS

443

Hyper Text Transfer Protocol Secure, the protocol that is widely used now for returning webpages that are encrypted with SSL/TLS. A green padlock icon with https:// means it is using HTTPS.

FTP

21

File Transfer Protocol. A protocol for transferring files from devices using wired or wireless. Considered not secure by today’s standards, and a new protocol (SFTP) was developed to replace it.

SMTP

25

Simple Mail Transfer Protocol, a protocol for sending emails on an email client such as Outlook or Gmail. Note this is only for sending, not receiving or reading emails.

IMAP

143

Internet Message Access Protocol, a type of email protocol where emails are viewable from a server but not stored on the computer. The opposite of this is POP3 (where emails are downloaded to your computer and will be deleted from the server)

DNS

53

Domain Name System, a system where domain names (like google.com) can be traced to the corresponding IP address. This is to make it so people don’t have to type IP addresses for websites they want to visit.

RDP

3389

Remote Desktop Protocol, a protocol for accessing and controlling a computer using a different computer typically from a remote location. Used by Windows Remote Desktop Protocol and many other RD apps.

SSH

22

Secure Shell is considered the successor to Telnet. SSH is used to access terminal commands and use them remotely on other devices. It is encrypted and secure unlike Telnet.

    1. 4.6Describe Domain Name Resolution Protocols  

HOSTS File – A text file within the operating system that maps domain names to IP addresses, it is checked before global DNS is run and it also overrides global DNS.

LMHOSTS File – A file that resolves NetBIOS host names to IP addresses. NetBIOS is a windows service that helps communication over networks.

These two files are used for static name resolution, the following terms are part of dynamic name resolution.

Windows Internet Name Service (WINS) – An old name resolution service that resolves NetBIOS names to IP addresses.

Domain Name System (DNS) – A system of servers that allow domain names to be resolved to IP addresses. This means that when someone wants to visit a website, they simply type in the easy to recognize domain URL (google.com, gmetrix.net, vbschools.com) rather than remembering the IPv4 addresses of their servers.

Top-Level Domain – This refers to the last part of a URL, it is an extension after a website’s name. Examples of TLDs are .com, .edu, .org, .gov.

Second-Level Domain – This refers to the part before the top-level domain, like the “google” in google.com or the “gmetrix” in gmetrix.net.

Third-Level Domain – This refers to a prefix to a second-level domain, used for finding certain mail servers and parts of websites. Examples are “docs” in docs.google.com, www, blog.example.com.

How DNS Works – When a user types in a domain name into the address bar, the browser first checks the computer’s DNS cache for an IP address, if it can’t find one, it asks the resolver server for the IP address. The resolver server is a server of your ISP that checks its own DNS cache for an answer, if none is found, it goes to a root server. A root server is one of the 13 servers around the world that hosts information about Top-Level Domains; a root server does not know what the IP address is, but it knows where you can go to find it. The root server directs the resolver server to the Top-Level Domain server corresponding to the URL typed into the address bar. (typing in google.com would bring back the .com TLD server) Once again, the TLD server does not know what the exact IP address is, so it sends the resolver to an Authoritative Name Server. The ANS actually does have the IP address you’re looking for, so it sends it to the resolver. After all of this, the IP address is stored temporarily in the DNS cache of the resolver and the computer being used.

I hope this is good enough and readable enough, credit to PowerCert for the info.

DNS Records – DNS records are stored in a zone file of an authoritative name server, which are the servers that know everything about a certain domain. There are many DNS records that you should know about.

DNS Record Name

Description and Function

A

The A record simply resolves the domain name to an IPv4 address. It also contains the Time to Live, which is a value in seconds and means “how long is a DNS result cached for?”

example.com = 23.215.0.136 TTL 120

AAAA

The AAAA record, pronounced as “quad A,” is a record that resolves the domain name to an IPv6 address and holds a TTL value.

example.com = 2001:0db8::3257:9652 TTL 7200

MX

The MX (Mail Exchanger) record points to an email server of a website. Sending an email to Terry@snk.com would point your email client to send the email to mail.snk.com which would be the mail server in this case. MX typically has two entries, a primary and secondary email server, with priority levels. It also holds a TTL value. A lower priority number means it is higher priority.

 

Priority 10 snk.com = mail.snk.com TTL 120

Priority 20 snk.com = mail2.snk.com TTL 200

PTR

The PTR (Pointer) record is the opposite of the A or AAAA record. It resolves an IP address to a domain name. So, if you type in an IP address you will be sent to the domain. It is actually used for email servers to be able to verify a sender is actually from the domain it says it’s from. If an email from Gmail has the correct IP address, the PTR record will check the IP address and match it to Gmail’s domain.

SRV

The SRV (Service) record resolves a domain name to a server and includes a port number with it. An example would be being redirected to the email server with port 143 for SMTP.

CNAME

The CNAME (Canonical Name) resolves a domain name to another domain name. It allows for a domain name to have an alias. A common use is that when someone types in www.example.com, it will be redirected to example.com; both names point to the same website.

SOA

The SOA (Start of Authority) stores administrative information about a DNS zone. DNS zones are segments of a network under a subdomain (shop.example.com, support.example.com) that have an administrator assigned to it. This record typically has an MNAME which is the primary name server, the RNAME is the email address of the administrator assigned to the DNS zone.

NS

The NS (Name Server) record provides the name of the authoritative name server of a domain.

Forward DNS Lookup – A forward DNS lookup is getting an IP address from a domain, like if you type in google.com you get Google’s IP address.

Reverse DNS Lookup – A reverse DNS lookup is getting a domain name from an IP address, such as typing 23.215.0.136 it will return example.com

    1. 4.7Identify the roles of networking services 

DHCP – Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol gives devices an IP address automatically from a DHCP server. This can be configured in many ways, usually based on a subnet mask and range. A DHCP server will give you the IP address 192.168.1.23 one day and 192.168.1.26 another, depending on devices connected to the router. If your computer has an IP address in the 169.254.0.1 to 169.254.255.254 range, that means you have an APIPA address and therefore the DHCP server is down. APIPA stands for Automatic Private IP addressing.

NAT – Network Address Translation allows for private IP addresses to be translated to one public IP address that can be routed to the Internet. It will take 192.168.1.2 for example and make it 73.24.0.32, which would be an IP address given by an ISP for this purpose.

Static vs Dynamic NAT – Static NAT means that every computer in a network connecting to the Internet uses the same public translated IP address. Dynamic NAT uses a pool of public IP addresses on a first come first served basis.

PAT – Port Address Translation is a type of NAT that allows users to share the same public IP address, using different ports for each user of the address.

Firewall – A firewall can be a software-based solution or a dedicated hardware solution. It is a device or service that filters network traffic, allowing trusted and safe connections and packets and blocking suspicious packets. There are two types, stateless and stateful. A stateless firewall checks each packet individually and filters them one by one, while a stateful firewall checks a packet, and monitors further packets being sent as an active connection. In a sense, it is using the session layer as a part of its filtering. Software-based firewalls usually take up more system resources than hardware-based ones.

  1. 5.Troubleshooting 

    1. 5.1Given a scenario, describe the troubleshooting process in a small-medium business network 

Troubleshooting Method – This exam objective isn’t very descriptive or specific. I will explain the CompTIA troubleshooting methodology, as that will be important for multiple CompTIA exams and is similar in effect to what Pearson wants you to know. As for “etiquette/professional conduct” I would say that the best choice of action is to remain professional in your speech and make decisions that keep a positive rapport with your clients without doing something that will affect your company’s money (such as offering handouts or something like that.

  1. 1.Identify the problem: 

  • Gather information. 

  • Question users. 

  • Identify symptoms. 

  • Determine if anything has changed. 

  • Duplicate the problem, if possible. 

  • Approach multiple problems individually. 

  1. 2.Research a knowledge base 

    • Internet (Online forums) 

    • Manufacturer Websites 

    • User Manuals 

  2. 3.Establish a theory of probable cause: 

  • Question the obvious. 

  • Consider multiple approaches: 

  • Top-to-bottom/bottom-to-top OSI model. 

  • Divide and conquer. 

  1. 4.Test the theory to determine cause: 

  • If theory is confirmed, determine next steps to resolve problem. 

  • If theory is not confirmed, establish new theory or escalate. 

  1. 5.Establish a plan of action to resolve the problem and identify potential effects. 

  2. 6.Implement the solution or escalate as necessary. 

  3. 7.Verify full system functionality and implement preventive measures if applicable. 

  4. 8.Document findings, actions, outcomes, and lessons learned throughout the process. 

More on the troubleshooting method at comptia.org/blog/troubleshooting-methodology

    1. 5.2Given a scenario, use the appropriate hardware troubleshooting tools 

Appropriate tool selection – It is always important to know what tools to use for what job. For example, if you are told there is a faulty ethernet cable, then you will want a cable tester and maybe a crimper to make a new cable. Use the context of the issue reports or support tickets and bring anything you think you will need. Failure to select the right tools wastes your time and the time of others and can cause further problems and damage to hardware if the wrong tool is used.

Multimeter – A multimeter is a measuring tool that measures electrical properties such as voltage, resistance, and current. It can be used to test if cables are able to transmit electrical signals, or if an outlet is functioning as it should.

Cable Tester – A cable tester is used test if a network cable is wired correctly and can support required bandwidth and throughput requirements of the cable category standard. Some advanced cable testers can tell you what is wrong with the cable, such as crossed wires or split pairs, and it can also the length of a cable and if it uses PoE. Cable testers could also be used to determine crosstalk and noise levels.

Toner – A toner, tone generator, or a “fox and hound” is a device that has two parts: a tone generator (fox) and a probe (hound.) The tone generator creates a signal throughout the cable or wall plate you’re testing, and the probe emits a sound that increases in volume the closer you get to the cable. It is used to trace cabling to find out what leads to where and to identify ports and connectors that may be unlabeled. Another use for a toner is that it can potentially find problems such as a complete break in a cable, but it cannot directly find faults or distance to them.

Time Domain Reflectometer (TDR) – A TDR is basically an advanced Toner and Cable Tester. It connects to a cable and sends an electrical pulse down the length of it, and when it encounters resistance or a fault, it determines the distance to the fault and the type of fault that occurred by reflections of the signal back to the device. It is used on metallic cabling like twisted pair copper and coaxial cables.

Optical TDR (OTDR) – An optical TDR is the version of a TDR used for exclusively fiber optic cables. It uses light signals instead of electrical signals to determine faults. Like a traditional TDR, an OTDR shows you the type of fault, the distance to the fault through analyzing reflections of the light pulse that go back to the device.

    1. 5.3Given a scenario, use the appropriate Windows software tools to troubleshoot a problem 

Appropriate tool selection – This exam mostly requires you to know windows command prompt commands and networking tools. Unlike OD-103 or OD-102, which do require you to know programs like disk management and device manager, as well as control panel and windows settings.

Ping – Uses ICMP to verify that your computer can connect to the desired computer. It sends echo requests in the form of packets (4 by default) and waits for a response from the IP address that you are pinging. At the end it displays the resulting packet loss (0% if all packets were received and sent back) The parameters to know are /n which specifies the number of packets to send, /l that determines the length or size of packets being sent if you want to change it from 32 bytes, /a will return a domain or host name from the IP address, using DNS or DNS cache, and /t will continuously ping the target until you want it to stop, when you press Ctrl + C.

Ipconfig – The ipconfig command tells you all the information about your network configurations and is also used for displaying DNS and DHCP settings as well as resetting them. An especially useful parameter to know are /all, which displays all adapters and more detailed information and specifics, such as a device MAC address, DHCP information, and DNS info. More parameters are /displaydns, which shows the DNS cache, /flushdns, which clears the DNS cache, and /release and /renew, which removes your current DHCP IP address and gives you another one respectively.

Tracert – Tracert (trace route) is a command that shows all the routers that your packets hop through before reaching the destination. It is a way to trace the route that your data takes to reach its destination. Useful parameters are /h which specifies the maximum number of hops in the search (default 30) and /d which stops the command from returning host names with the IP addresses, which could potentially make the command go faster.

Pathping – The pathping command is like a combination of tracert and ping. It provides latency reports and packet loss during intermediate hops between the source and destination. It pings each intermediate router for a more conclusive and detailed diagnosis of the route.

Nslookup – The nslookup command displays DNS information about an IP address or domain name. You can also use it to return certain DNS records instead of just the A record, like if you want to know the MX record for google, for example.

Hostname – Returns the host name of your computer, that’s it.

Netstat – Shows the current open and established TCP connections to your computer. The parameter /a shows all TCP connections as well as TCP and UDP ports that are open. /p specifies which protocol you want to know the connections for, so /p UDP shows all UDP connections.

Arp – The arp command resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses on a LAN. It displays the ARP cache. You must run arp with a parameter such as /a which shows all arp cache information. You cannot just type arp. ARP stands for Address Resolution Protocol.

 

    1. 5.4Given a scenario, use the appropriate Linux software tools to troubleshoot a problem 

Appropriate tool selection – Linux commands are pretty similar to Windows, but you may get questions such as “what commands on a Linux device are used for network troubleshooting” and you would have to pick out the ones that are Linux specific. It is good to know these anyways because a network technician will most likely have to use Linux at some point. Also Linux is a kernel, not an operating system, many operating systems are built from Linux and have differences in their commands, I assume the most popular Linux distributions like Ubuntu are the basis for this objective.

Ping – The ping command in Linux is the same as the Windows ping command, you can check connectivity to a host by sending ICMP data packets and getting a result. It also tells you the minimum, average, and maximum response times just like Windows.

Ip (ip addr) – The IP command in Linux has many uses, including altering routing tables and changing the default gateway and even modifying interfaces. The main function you need to know is that ip addr is the Linux version of ipconfig. It shows all the connection interfaces and information about them.

Traceroute – This command is tracert in Linux. You can use it to see the hops between your computer and the destination. It also can send more packet types than just ICMP, including TCP and UDP packets.

Tracepath        - Tracepath is similar to traceroute but it has an emphasis on the MTU, which shows the maximum size of a packet across a network without being fragmented. It also mostly does not require admin privileges while traceroute does. Tracepath is a simpler version of traceroute, as traceroute has many advanced options.

Dig – The dig command is similar to nslookup in Windows, it allows you to retrieve IP addresses from host names and domain names, and even allows you to get specific records. Dig is more advanced than nslookup and allows for much more customization in queries and results you want.

Host – Host allows for simple DNS lookups. It is similar to dig, but easier to use at the cost of customization and utility.

Netstat – This command is the same as it is in Windows. It allows you to see your current TCP and UDP connections and ports that are open.

Arp – Similar to the Windows command, it allows you to view the Address Resolution Protocol Cache.

Exam Tips and Conclusion

I created this study guide before I took the exam and I passed while working on it. I would say that the test was easier than the GMetrix practices, but that might just be because I learned things over the GMetrix and then used it on the test. I can’t recommend enough passing all three networking practice exams on GMetrix, as it is pretty accurate to the exam. There are multiple choice questions: some have single answers, some have two answers that you must pick, and some have multiple answers that have yes and no buttons you must pick (usually this has to do with true or false statements.) There are drop down questions where you must choose from a list, which can be used when the test wants you to correct a change in a statement or fill in a blank. Some questions have a picture of command prompt and you must diagnose the problem with the network, this type of question also uses a drop down answer choice.

Some tips I live by when taking exams are: Read the entire question!! When I was doing the GMetrix, I started memorizing the questions and answers to the point I would just pick the right answer without even reading the question. Reading the full question is crucial to understanding what you must do to answer correctly (I often found myself picking only one answer when the question asks for two, or I choose a true statement when it asks me to pick the false statement. You have plenty of time for this exam, trust me, so read the question carefully and even read it back out loud to better understand it. Another tip is advice from Mr. G that he told everyone in my class when we took the CompTIA ITF+: Go with your first answer choice! Often this exam (and many others in life) have two choices that really both could be right or seem like viable choices, but it is better to just stick with the answer you chose unless you absolutely know your first choice was wrong. I often flag questions I do not recall the exact answers to, but I usually just submit without going back so I don’t end up second guessing myself and spending too much time on one question. One last thing: relax and focus. You always have another try if you fail. Don’t get distracted while testing, and also do not overprepare. I spent 6 months preparing for the ITF+ when in reality it was really easy, and as a result I did not have enough time to study for Network+ (I am a senior at the time of writing this, so I can’t come back another year.)

 

Here are some statistics for this guide, which may or may not matter to someone.

I created this guide on 5/15/2025 and finished it on 5/26/2025. I spent a total of 800 minutes, or 13 hours over those 11 days writing this guide. It has 30 pages and 10,984 words. This was written for the intention of future students reading it. If you are reading this, good luck, and congratulations on getting to the end.

-Harper L. 5/26/2025 @ 12:40 AM
